Analyze this Huntress EDR incident report and extract every Indicator of Compromise:

Investigative Summary
---------------------
Evidence suggests that at 2025-08-06 12:29 UTC, the SimpleHelp instance on this endpoint was used to create a Defender exclusion for the 'C:\' drive. 

Huntress also observed extensive enumeration of the endpoint carried out via the SimpleHelp instance. 

This SimpleHelp instance is only present on this host within the organisation and is configured to communicate with the following domain: 
- hxxp[://]help[.]hotlinesinc[.]com

Due to the potential severity of this incident, this host has been isolated to prevent further ingress. 

Huntress has the following recommendations: 
- Consider implementing your Incident Response procedures to fully scope the incident as attackers will often move to other hosts on the network.
- Review internal log sources to identify potential unauthorized access.
- Review compromised hosts, and if applicable, Active Directory, for any unauthorized accounts.
- Consider resetting passwords for all potentially impacted accounts.
- Run a full system scan to identify anything real-time scanning may not have identified.
- Ensure that all security products are running and correctly configured as expected on affected endpoints as well all possible hosts to increase visibility into potential threat actor activity.
- Wipe any affected hosts and reinstall from a known good baseline.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Rogue Simplehelp: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious Simplehelp installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enrolling the user in security awareness training to prevent recurrence.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105293/infection_reports/1631471#remediations-tab

Extract all indicators of compromise from this MDR security report:

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-07-09 04:07:55 UTC, Huntress observed user "shop2" (S-1-5-21-2196596155-207489825-4145404214-1314) executing a malicious enumeration-based command via nltest.exe & net.exe. Prior to this activity, the user can be seen establishing a remote desktop session from the "fe80::7bba:26ff:8c4e:d002" IP address at "2025-07-09 03:59:04 UTC." The user logged off after carrying out the enumeration commands.

Enumeration Commands Run:
- net.exe group "Domain Computers" /domain
- nltest.exe /dclist:

We have observed no additional activity from this user except for the enumeration commands. This activity is suspicious and warrants further investigation by your organisation. If this activity is unexpected, we recommend that you engage your incident response procedures. We have raised this report for situational awareness purposes.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/104805/infection_reports/1591516#remediations-tab

Analyze this Huntress EDR incident report and extract every Indicator of Compromise:

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
Evidence suggests that at 2025-10-30 17:28:37 UTC, the user “craigw” (SID: S-1-5-21-3890320951-2591504539-3288043570-1172) interacted with a fake CAPTCHA hosted on a malicious advertisement, which appeared to originate from a Reddit domain redirect.

The malicious domain — hxxps[://]55i[.]j3ve[.]ru/clh1ygiq — hosted a PowerShell downloader that fetched and executed malicious payloads. These payloads subsequently injected a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) via the process MSBUILD.exe.

Further investigation confirms that the RAT is actively communicating with the external IP 46[.]62[.]206[.]119, which is associated with malicious activity and potential command-and-control infrastructure.

Remediation Recommendations:

-Immediately block outbound communication to 46[.]62[.]206[.]119 at the firewall and endpoint levels.

-Review proxy and DNS logs for any other systems attempting to contact this IP or related domains.

-Reset credentials for the affected user (craigw) and any accounts used on the compromised host.

-Enforce MFA on all accounts if not already enabled.

Domain:
hxxps[://]55i[.]j3ve[.]ru/clh1ygiq 

Indication Of Compromised:
Remote Access Trojan reaching out to IP 46[.]62[.]206[.]119

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Malicious Downloader / Malicious Download: Malicious activity has resulted in the download and execution of an additional malicious payload(s). 

Remote Access Trojan (RAT): A Remote Access Trojan (RAT) facilitates a threat actor's malicious remote control and persistent to access to a machine.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Kill Process - path: C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework\v4.0.30319\MSBuild.exe + pid: 3868
- Reboot the Host - remediation: A reboot is required to complete the remediation plan

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Immediately block outbound communication to 46[.]62[.]206[.]119 at the firewall and endpoint levels.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Reset credentials for the affected user (craigw) and any accounts used on the compromised host.
- Enforce MFA on all accounts if not already enabled.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105204/infection_reports/1775239#remediations-tab

Parse the following security incident report and return all IOCs in structured JSON:

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
At 2025-12-06 19:36:17 UTC, Huntress observed user “FatherMarkSuslenko" executing a malicious process “em_UoUcNGOh_installer_Win7-Win11_x86_x64 (4).msi". Evidence suggests that this process is a dropper that, once executed, installs malicious Remote Management Tools, including "ITArian".

These tools, on their own, are typically not malicious; however, context suggests that they are, in fact, rogue RMM tools that were installed in the following directories. This would likely result in the execution and installation of a Rogue ScreenConnect instance on the host, which would be configured to communicate with adversarial infrastructure; however, this has not been observed at this time.

We have isolated the endpoint due to the suspicious nature of this activity. We recommend removing all files and directories associated with the two tools, reimaging the host to establish a secure baseline, and enrolling the end user in Security Awareness training.

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete Service - name: RmmService
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\RmmService
- Delete File - path: c:\program files (x86)\itarian\endpoint manager\rmmservice.exe
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\webrtc-plugin.dll
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\libGLESV2.dll
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\libEGL.dll
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\icudtl.dat
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\enrollment_settings.ini
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\edr-plugin.dll
- Delete File - path: C:\Program Files (x86)\ITarian\Endpoint Manager\\d3dcompiler_47.dll
- + 61 more

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Enroll the user in a security awareness training session to reinforce safe practices in the corporate environment.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/229303/infection_reports/1844911#remediations-tab

Review this security report and extract ALL network, file, process, and persistence IOCs:

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
At 2025-10-25 05:12 UTC, Huntress observed domain enumeration commands executed under the user context of Xerox (SID: S-1-5-21-2262026380-2319951226-2254446633-1512) on host ATN44023. 

The activity originated from the internal IP address 10.11.50[.]100. This IP corresponds to a workstation, DESKTOP-DBB1FMG, which has been observed authenticating the Xerox account as far back as 2025-10-24. The absence of a Huntress Agent on the source host limits visibility into the full extent of the user's activity.

The activity involves the execution of Windows' built-in utility, nltest.exe, with parameters commonly used for domain reconnaissance.

Observed commands:
C:\WINDOWS\system32\nltest.exe" /dclist:
C:\WINDOWS\system32\nltest.exe" /domain_Trusts /all_trusts

The nltest utility is a legitimate Windows command-line tool used for network and trust relationship testing. However, its use to list Domain Controllers (/dclist) and enumerate all domain trusts (/domain_Trusts /all_trusts) is a common tactic employed by threat actors post-compromise to map the Active Directory (AD) environment. 

This reconnaissance activity suggests the user account is determining the network topology to facilitate lateral movement and identify potential high-value targets, such as Domain Controllers. The lack of a Huntress Agent on the source IP/workstation 10.11.50[.]100 makes it impossible to definitively confirm the full context of the user's actions or determine the initial compromise vector. Due to the nature of the commands and the risk of further pivoting, the host has been isolated to contain the threat.

Recommendations:
----------------------------------
- Confirm if this activity is performed by a known system administrator and is part of routine maintenance. 
- If unexpected, reset the password for the Xerox account and force logouts for any active sessions. 
- Review all activity associated with the Xerox account on all devices since at least 2025-10-24.
- Review the isolated host for any other persistence mechanisms or staging folders left by the threat actor.

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/103323/infection_reports/1766467#remediations-tab

Extract all indicators of compromise from this MDR security report:

Investigative Summary
---------------------
On 2025-09-25 at 04:07:28 UTC, Huntress detected a malicious ScreenConnect wscript executing from the temporary directory on the device "Edward-W10". This ScreenConnect instance was confirmed to be malicious and was observed reaching out to the domain ind.erdemliecotourism[.]com. At the same time, Huntress noted the concurrent installation of a malicious PDQ RMM tool that was installed on 2025-08-25 at 19:10:17 UTC. 

Further analysis, we noticed multiple RMM tools added to the device. This includes the following:
- JWrapper-Remote Access.
- SimpleHelp.
- CentraStage.
- RemotePC.

As part of the investigation into the original activity, Huntress conducted static analysis on the file "C:\Users\Edward\Documents\ConnectWiseControl\Temp\Steepwe.js". This revealed an obfuscated script, which we successfully decoded. The script was found to point to another file on the endpoint located at:
"C:\Users\Edward\AppData\Roaming\Wriedrepr"

This second file was also obfuscated. After Huntress decoded it, we determined that it made a malicious GET request to the following domain:
http[://]drive[.]sourcekity-cloud.io/c3/Abo.ktf

We recommend you wipe this device and restore it from a known good backup. 

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Rogue ScreenConnect: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious ScreenConnect (ConnectWise Control) installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host. 

Malicious Downloader / Malicious Download: Malicious activity has resulted in the download and execution of an additional malicious payload(s). 

Malicious Script: A malicious script is a type of code that can be used to harm a computer without the user's knowledge. Malicious scripts are a type of malicious code, which is any computer programming or web scripting that is designed to cause damage or create security holes.

Suspicious App Usage: Huntress has detected a suspicious enterprise application added to this tenant.

Suspicious RMM: A suspicious remote monitoring and management (RMM) application was detected on this endpoint. These applications may be benign within the environment, or levied by adversaries as a method of persistence. Occasionally software vendors will additionally distribute these RMM's as a portion of their support process. 

Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Rogue Simplehelp: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious Simplehelp installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete Service - name: ScreenConnect Client (b76e1aee72eb16ed)
- Delete Service - name: PDQConnectAgent
- Delete Service - name: Remote Access Service
- Delete Service - name: ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141)
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (b76e1aee72eb16ed)
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\PDQConnectAgent
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Remote Access Service
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141)
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Edward\Documents\ConnectWiseControl\Temp\Steepwe.js
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Edward\AppData\Roaming\Wriedrepr
- + 5 more

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Enroll the user in a security awareness training session to reinforce safe practices in the corporate environment.
- Audit host/environment and ensure this application is allowed and anticipated on this host.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Remove the application from the tenant.
- Audit MFA devices for the identity.
- Audit the identity for administrator roles.
- Audit for malicious inbox rules and forwards.
- Audit for malicious OAuth grants.
- Audit the admin audit log for malicious activity.

Investigative Summary
---------------------
Evidence suggests that at 2025-08-06 12:29 UTC, the SimpleHelp instance on this endpoint was used to create a Defender exclusion for the 'C:\' drive. 

Huntress also observed extensive enumeration of the endpoint carried out via the SimpleHelp instance. 

This SimpleHelp instance is only present on this host within the organisation and is configured to communicate with the following domain: 
- hxxp[://]help[.]hotlinesinc[.]com

Due to the potential severity of this incident, this host has been isolated to prevent further ingress. 

Huntress has the following recommendations: 
- Consider implementing your Incident Response procedures to fully scope the incident as attackers will often move to other hosts on the network.
- Review internal log sources to identify potential unauthorized access.
- Review compromised hosts, and if applicable, Active Directory, for any unauthorized accounts.
- Consider resetting passwords for all potentially impacted accounts.
- Run a full system scan to identify anything real-time scanning may not have identified.
- Ensure that all security products are running and correctly configured as expected on affected endpoints as well all possible hosts to increase visibility into potential threat actor activity.
- Wipe any affected hosts and reinstall from a known good baseline.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Rogue Simplehelp: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious Simplehelp installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enrolling the user in security awareness training to prevent recurrence.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105293/infection_reports/1631471#remediations-tab

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
Since this is a newly onboarded host, this is likely from a previous incident as "ScreenConnect Client (6100c8e237f6b876)" appears to have been on the host since 2024 as indicated by its creation time in directory: 'C:\Program Files (x86)\ScreenConnect Client (6100c8e237f6b876)'

At 2026-01-08 15:20:11 UTC, a Windows Service with the name "ScreenConnect Client (6100c8e237f6b876)" was detected on the endpoint. Evidence indicates prior to this host having the Huntress agent installed, there was a malicious ScreenConnect instance on the host which reaches out to domain: 38[.]69[.]8[.]156 over port 8041. Huntress also observes there were active IP addresses communicating via port 8041, which were 50[.]225[.]154[.]136 and 38[.]69[.]8[.]156.

ScreenConnect is a legitimate remote access tool used by IT professionals for support and management, and because it gives full remote control it can be very useful for authorized administration. However, its same remote access capabilities are frequently leveraged by threat actors—whether via compromised accounts, phishing lures, or trojanized installers—to gain unauthorized control of systems once inside a network. Because of this dual-use nature, its deployment and use should always be authorized and monitored.

We recommend immediately scanning the affected host for additional malware, reviewing network logs for connections to "50[.]225[.]154[.]136 and 38[.]69[.]8[.]156", adding these IPs to your organization's firewall blacklist, and providing security awareness training to the user regarding social engineering tactics. The user's credentials should be considered potentially compromised and may require reset depending on the payload's capabilities. It is recommended to reimage the host from known-good media for full remediation. 

This host has been isolated to prevent further ingress.

Please refer to remediation tasks for next steps.

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Rogue ScreenConnect: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious ScreenConnect (ConnectWise Control) installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host. 

Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Persistence : Persistence consists of techniques that adversaries use to keep access to systems across restarts, changed credentials, and other interruptions that could cut off their access. Techniques used for persistence include any access, action, or configuration changes that let them maintain their foothold on systems, such as replacing or hijacking legitimate code or adding startup code.

Prior Compromise: The security incident on this host occurred prior to the Huntress agent installation. We will attempt to address any observed malicious behavior, but there may be unreported changes to this host that will not be detected due to limited telemetry.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\System32\WScript.exe + pid: 18884
- Delete Service - name: ScreenConnect Client (6100c8e237f6b876)
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (6100c8e237f6b876)
- Delete File - path: c:\program files (x86)\screenconnect client (6100c8e237f6b876)\screenconnect.clientservice.exe + sha256: f1b5c000794f046259121c63ed37f9eff0cfe1258588eca6fd85e16d3922767e
- Reboot the Host - remediation: A reboot is required to complete the remediation plan

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Enroll the user in a security awareness training session to reinforce safe practices in the corporate environment.
- Reimage or restore to a clean baseline if the compromise is extensive or trust in device integrity can't be restored.
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/573692/infection_reports/1876640#remediations-tab

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
At 2025-10-25 05:12 UTC, Huntress observed domain enumeration commands executed under the user context of Xerox (SID: S-1-5-21-2262026380-2319951226-2254446633-1512) on host ATN44023. 

The activity originated from the internal IP address 10.11.50[.]100. This IP corresponds to a workstation, DESKTOP-DBB1FMG, which has been observed authenticating the Xerox account as far back as 2025-10-24. The absence of a Huntress Agent on the source host limits visibility into the full extent of the user's activity.

The activity involves the execution of Windows' built-in utility, nltest.exe, with parameters commonly used for domain reconnaissance.

Observed commands:
C:\WINDOWS\system32\nltest.exe" /dclist:
C:\WINDOWS\system32\nltest.exe" /domain_Trusts /all_trusts

The nltest utility is a legitimate Windows command-line tool used for network and trust relationship testing. However, its use to list Domain Controllers (/dclist) and enumerate all domain trusts (/domain_Trusts /all_trusts) is a common tactic employed by threat actors post-compromise to map the Active Directory (AD) environment. 

This reconnaissance activity suggests the user account is determining the network topology to facilitate lateral movement and identify potential high-value targets, such as Domain Controllers. The lack of a Huntress Agent on the source IP/workstation 10.11.50[.]100 makes it impossible to definitively confirm the full context of the user's actions or determine the initial compromise vector. Due to the nature of the commands and the risk of further pivoting, the host has been isolated to contain the threat.

Recommendations:
----------------------------------
- Confirm if this activity is performed by a known system administrator and is part of routine maintenance. 
- If unexpected, reset the password for the Xerox account and force logouts for any active sessions. 
- Review all activity associated with the Xerox account on all devices since at least 2025-10-24.
- Review the isolated host for any other persistence mechanisms or staging folders left by the threat actor.

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/103323/infection_reports/1766467#remediations-tab

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-06-25 17:44:30 UTC, Huntress identified a prior infection which left a persistence mechanism as the malicious Windows Service with the name “C__Users_tabadmin_AppData_Local_70_ExtractedInstaller.exe", on the newly registered host "PROSHOP6".

This service when executed spawns the malicious command “C:\Users\tabadmin\AppData\Local\70_ExtractedInstaller.exe /exenoupdates  /prereqs "1,2,3,4,6"".

Please see the remediation section for next steps.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Persistence : Persistence consists of techniques that adversaries use to keep access to systems across restarts, changed credentials, and other interruptions that could cut off their access. Techniques used for persistence include any access, action, or configuration changes that let them maintain their foothold on systems, such as replacing or hijacking legitimate code or adding startup code.

Prior Compromise: The security incident on this host occurred prior to the Huntress agent installation. We will attempt to address any observed malicious behavior, but there may be unreported changes to this host that will not be detected due to limited telemetry.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete File - path: C:\WINDOWS\System32\Tasks\C__Users_tabadmin_AppData_Local_70_ExtractedInstaller.exe
- Delete File - path: c:\windows\system32\tasks\c__users_tabadmin_appdata_local_70_extractedinstaller.exe

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident.

On 2025-11-19 03:14 UTC, Huntress observed the execution of a mouse-hiding utility, Hide-Mouse-on-blankscreen.exe, on ATN68139. This utility was launched through a potentially malicious ScreenConnect Client instance (ID: 1132a4b096a44934), which reportedly was installed on or around 2025-10-29  UTC based on the config files creation date. 

The presence of the obfuscation utility, deployed via a potentially malicious remote access tool, strongly suggests that a threat actor is actively managing their session and attempting to conceal activity from local users or monitoring tools.

When reviewing the Screen Connect instances configured on this host, it appears that two are currently in operation:
- ScreenConnect Client (1132a4b096a44934) - relay.willisterwinpress[.]net=45.143.146[.]80
- ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141) - screenconnect.patroldog[.]com

This activity is highly suspicious and suggests the user may have fallen for a phishing attempt, providing an unauthenticated threat actor with remote access to the system. This type of initial access is often a precursor to reconnaissance, privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or the deployment of ransomware.

The system is currently not responsive and potentially offline, limiting further analysis at this time.

-----------------

SHA256: 3b4ce39ae45d42dc3ed0160db65b90aff4e8d2b4b760ee636315d9814ae056bf

User Accounts:
-----------------
UserName: ATN68139\Pete
SID: S-1-5-21-3452951620-765747871-1129988434-1002

Recommendations:
-----------------
- Remove Malicious Client: Investigate and uninstall the malicious ScreenConnect Client (ID: 1132a4b096a44934) and remove the deployed mouse-hiding utility.
- Review All Clients: Review all other ScreenConnect instances (including ID: 24a22b9fc261d141) to confirm they are authorised and legitimate, and ensure all remote access software on the host is secure.
- Enforce an immediate password reset for ATN68139\Pete across all linked systems

Investigative Summary
---------------------
On 2025-09-25 at 04:07:28 UTC, Huntress detected a malicious ScreenConnect wscript executing from the temporary directory on the device "Edward-W10". This ScreenConnect instance was confirmed to be malicious and was observed reaching out to the domain ind.erdemliecotourism[.]com. At the same time, Huntress noted the concurrent installation of a malicious PDQ RMM tool that was installed on 2025-08-25 at 19:10:17 UTC. 

Further analysis, we noticed multiple RMM tools added to the device. This includes the following:
- JWrapper-Remote Access.
- SimpleHelp.
- CentraStage.
- RemotePC.

As part of the investigation into the original activity, Huntress conducted static analysis on the file "C:\Users\Edward\Documents\ConnectWiseControl\Temp\Steepwe.js". This revealed an obfuscated script, which we successfully decoded. The script was found to point to another file on the endpoint located at:
"C:\Users\Edward\AppData\Roaming\Wriedrepr"

This second file was also obfuscated. After Huntress decoded it, we determined that it made a malicious GET request to the following domain:
http[://]drive[.]sourcekity-cloud.io/c3/Abo.ktf

We recommend you wipe this device and restore it from a known good backup. 

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Rogue ScreenConnect: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious ScreenConnect (ConnectWise Control) installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host. 

Malicious Downloader / Malicious Download: Malicious activity has resulted in the download and execution of an additional malicious payload(s). 

Malicious Script: A malicious script is a type of code that can be used to harm a computer without the user's knowledge. Malicious scripts are a type of malicious code, which is any computer programming or web scripting that is designed to cause damage or create security holes.

Suspicious App Usage: Huntress has detected a suspicious enterprise application added to this tenant.

Suspicious RMM: A suspicious remote monitoring and management (RMM) application was detected on this endpoint. These applications may be benign within the environment, or levied by adversaries as a method of persistence. Occasionally software vendors will additionally distribute these RMM's as a portion of their support process. 

Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Rogue Simplehelp: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious Simplehelp installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete Service - name: ScreenConnect Client (b76e1aee72eb16ed)
- Delete Service - name: PDQConnectAgent
- Delete Service - name: Remote Access Service
- Delete Service - name: ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141)
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (b76e1aee72eb16ed)
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\PDQConnectAgent
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Remote Access Service
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141)
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Edward\Documents\ConnectWiseControl\Temp\Steepwe.js
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Edward\AppData\Roaming\Wriedrepr
- + 5 more

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:

Threat Descriptions:

Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-08-02 07:07:41UTC user "tabadmin" logged onto host 	SL-DC-01 from an internal IP address of 10.100.0[.]6 which is a Huntress unmanaged host.

At 2025-08-02 07:28 UTC, Huntress detected an adversary using "WinRAR.exe" to archive and password-protect numerous sensitive document types. This was part of a broader series of interactive commands, including reconnaissance with "whoami.exe" and an attempt to uninstall the Huntress agent. The presence of "rdpclip.exe" indicates the attacker was connected via a Remote Desktop session.

These actions are consistent with pre-ransomware activity. The threat actor accessed backup software, likely to disable or delete recovery points, and have download data exfiltration tool. The attempt to remove the Huntress agent, is a clear defence evasion tactic designed to prolong unauthorized access and evade detection.

The attacker has active control of this system, posing an immediate risk of widespread data theft and business disruption. If successful, the attacker could encrypt critical files, leak stolen data publicly, and demand a ransom, leading to significant operational and financial damage.

Huntress has mass isolated the organisation to prevent further compromise. Please carry out assisted remediation, rotate all user credentials and activate your incident response plan. If the mass isolation is lifted without determining the root cause of this intrusion, the threat actor access will maintain control over this system.

Technical Artefacts:
-------------------
- Compromised user: "SL\tabadmin (S-1-5-21-2554046153-4285503646-2850861276-5162)"
- File path: "c:\Program Files\WinRAR\WinRAR.exe"
- Command Line: `winrar.exe a -m0 -v3g -tn1000d -n*.txt -n*.pdf -n*.xls -n*.doc -n*.xlsx -n*.docx -hpctlobby[.]com "C:\DATA\done\DATA.rar" "C:\DATA"`
- File path: "C:\Windows\System32\CMD.exe"
- Command Line: `/C "whoami"`
- File path: "C:\Windows\System32\rdpclip.exe"
- File path: "E:\Program Files\Veeam\Backup and Replication\Console\veeam.backup.shell.exe"
- File path: "C:\Users\tabadmin\Downloads\WinRAR-713.exe"
- File path: "C:\Program Files\Huntress\Uninstall.exe"
- File path: "C:\Program Files\WinRAR\uninstall.exe"
- Command Line: `"C:\Program Files\WinRAR\uninstall.exe" /setup`
- File path: "C:\Windows\system32\whoami.exe"

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Exfiltration Tools: The adversary is using data exfiltration tools, which facilitate the malicious transfer of data out of the network. This is not evidence of successful exfiltration, and Huntress does not have insight on the affected files. 

Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Kill Process - path: c:\Program Files\WinRAR\WinRAR.exe + pid: 11600

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.

-Review proxy and DNS logs for any other systems attempting to contact this IP or related domains.

-Reset credentials for the affected user (craigw) and any accounts used on the compromised host.

-Enforce MFA on all accounts if not already enabled.

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
At 2025-10-29 13:37:34 UTC, Huntress observed user "Pete" executing a malicious RMM installer at `C:\Users\Pete\Downloads\modestparty.exe`. The installer was downloaded from `hxxps://3a62a61d[.]newmodestparty[.]pages[.]dev/`, which originated from a phishing email in the user's Yahoo mail inbox. The execution resulted in the deployment of an unauthorized GoTo Resolver RMM, which then reached out to retrieve a malicious ScreenConnect instance from `hxxps://server.willisterwinpress[.]net/Bin/ScreenConnect.ClientSetup.msi?e=Access&y=Guest`. We did not observe the successful installation of the ScreenConnect instance or any subsequent malicious activities on the host.

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Malicious Remote Management Tool: A legitimate remote management tool has been misused by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence and further exploit the network.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete Service - name: GoToResolve_295488703092847637

- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Pete\Downloads\modestparty (1).exe
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Pete\Downloads\modestparty (2).exe

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-08-21 22:32:16 UTC, Huntress observed the following users are compromised:
tabadmin (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1133)
jeanette (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1108)

The compromised user 'jeanette' appears to have logged in from the IP 192.168.10.50 (Host name: DESKTOP-K1PKL6P) to the endpoint ATN79684 at approximately 2025-08-21 19:28:27.

This user was then observed logging into the endpoint accounting-DAFF0JD at approximately 2025-08-21 22:04:08 and then utilized PSEXEC. Afterwards, this user gained access to the endpoint DEPCO-DC01 and accessed the tabadmin account. Cobalt Strike was utilized during this intrusion, in addition to disabling security features.

It is likely that the initial access occurred through SonicWall. Huntress recommends engaging with incident response as soon as possible in addition to the remediation section. Mass isolation has been deployed to prevent further malicious activity.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Privilege Escalation: Privilege Escalation consists of techniques that adversaries use to gain higher-level permissions on a system or network. Adversaries can often enter and explore a network with unprivileged access but require elevated permissions to follow through on their objectives. Common approaches are to take advantage of system weaknesses, misconfigurations, and vulnerabilities. Examples of elevated access include:  SYSTEM/root level local administrator user account with admin-like access user accounts with access to specific system or perform specific function These techniques often overlap with Persistence techniques, as OS features that let an adversary persist can execute in an elevated context.

Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

Command and Control: Command and Control (C2) consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Defense Evasion: Defense Evasion consists of techniques that adversaries use to avoid detection throughout their compromise. Techniques used for defense evasion include uninstalling/disabling security software, clearing logs, or obfuscating/encrypting data and scripts. Adversaries also leverage and abuse trusted processes to hide and masquerade their malware. 

The Huntress SOC identified the Malware Family as Cobalt Strike.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\system32\cmd.exe + pid: 16288
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\System32\Conhost.exe + pid: 15268
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\SysWOW64\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe + pid: 9540
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\system32\runonce.exe + pid: 16312
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\system32\runonce.exe + pid: 16272
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\system32\runonce.exe + pid: 17180
- Kill Process - path: C:\WINDOWS\system32\runonce.exe + pid: 11308
- Delete Service - name: PSEXESVC
- Delete Registry Key - key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\PSEXESVC
- Delete File - path: c:\windows\psexesvc.exe

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.
- Reboot the host to terminate malicious processes & sever any potentially outstanding connections. 
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident. 
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.
- We recommend taking the following steps to respond to this incident: - Disable SSLVPN until this subset of remediations can be accomplished: - Ensure SonicOS is updated to version 7.3 or greater. - Rotate all credentials for all users on the SonicWall SSLVPN. - Audit organization LDAP SSLVPN Default User Groups configuration. - Disable/reenable TOTP/MFA for relevant accounts. - You can read more about this set of remediations here: https://www.sonicwall.com/support/notices/gen-7-and-newer-sonicwall-firewalls-sslvpn-recent-threat-activity/250804095336430

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105150/infection_reports/1659493#remediations-tab

Afterwards, this user gained access to the endpoint DEPCO-DC01 and accessed the tabadmin account. Cobalt Strike was utilized during this intrusion, in addition to disabling security features.

It is likely that the initial access occurred through SonicWall. Huntress recommends engaging with incident response as soon as possible in addition to the remediation section. Mass isolation has been deployed to prevent further malicious activity.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Defense Evasion: Defense Evasion consists of techniques that adversaries use to avoid detection throughout their compromise. Techniques used for defense evasion include uninstalling/disabling security software, clearing logs, or obfuscating/encrypting data and scripts. Adversaries also leverage and abuse trusted processes to hide and masquerade their malware. 

Privilege Escalation: Privilege Escalation consists of techniques that adversaries use to gain higher-level permissions on a system or network. Adversaries can often enter and explore a network with unprivileged access but require elevated permissions to follow through on their objectives. Common approaches are to take advantage of system weaknesses, misconfigurations, and vulnerabilities. Examples of elevated access include:  SYSTEM/root level local administrator user account with admin-like access user accounts with access to specific system or perform specific function These techniques often overlap with Persistence techniques, as OS features that let an adversary persist can execute in an elevated context.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Command and Control: Command and Control (C2) consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Reboot the host to terminate malicious processes & sever any potentially outstanding connections. 
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident. 
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.
- We recommend taking the following steps to respond to this incident: - Disable SSLVPN until this subset of remediations can be accomplished: - Ensure SonicOS is updated to version 7.3 or greater. - Rotate all credentials for all users on the SonicWall SSLVPN. - Audit organization LDAP SSLVPN Default User Groups configuration. - Disable/reenable TOTP/MFA for relevant accounts. - You can read more about this set of remediations here: https://www.sonicwall.com/support/notices/gen-7-and-newer-sonicwall-firewalls-sslvpn-recent-threat-activity/250804095336430

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105150/infection_reports/1659496#remediations-tab

On 2025-08-07 at 14:05:01 UTC, a malicious Windows startup with the name "PP1" was detected on the endpoint "Timsassistant". This startup will execute a malicious NetSupport instance (C:\Users\JacobParks\AppData\Roaming\Dire\client32.exe) configured to communicate with the adversarial IP address "94.158.245[.]140" as a persistence mechanism. Available telemetry indicates that this startup was present prior to the installation of the Huntress agent, suggesting that this endpoint may have been previously compromised. No additional evidence of malice was observed within available data at the time of investigation.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Hidden NetSupport: NetSupport is a legitimate remote management tool that is misused by threat actors to gain unauthorized access and control of the reported endpoint. Threat actors do this to hide their presence, maintain persistence, and further exploit the network.

Persistence : Persistence consists of techniques that adversaries use to keep access to systems across restarts, changed credentials, and other interruptions that could cut off their access. Techniques used for persistence include any access, action, or configuration changes that let them maintain their foothold on systems, such as replacing or hijacking legitimate code or adding startup code.

Prior Compromise: The security incident on this host occurred prior to the Huntress agent installation. We will attempt to address any observed malicious behavior, but there may be unreported changes to this host that will not be detected due to limited telemetry.

Malware Artifact: A Malware Artifact is a piece of evidence left behind by malware on a computer system. It could be a file, registry entry, or network connection that indicates the presence of malicious activity.

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
On 2025-11-19 03:14 UTC, Huntress observed the execution of a mouse-hiding utility, Hide-Mouse-on-blankscreen.exe, on ATN68139. This utility was launched through a potentially malicious ScreenConnect Client instance (ID: 1132a4b096a44934), which reportedly was installed on or around 2025-10-29  UTC based on the config files creation date. 

The presence of the obfuscation utility, deployed via a potentially malicious remote access tool, strongly suggests that a threat actor is actively managing their session and attempting to conceal activity from local users or monitoring tools.

When reviewing the Screen Connect instances configured on this host, it appears that two are currently in operation:
- ScreenConnect Client (1132a4b096a44934) - relay.willisterwinpress[.]net=45.143.146[.]80
- ScreenConnect Client (24a22b9fc261d141) - screenconnect.patroldog[.]com

This activity is highly suspicious and suggests the user may have fallen for a phishing attempt, providing an unauthenticated threat actor with remote access to the system. This type of initial access is often a precursor to reconnaissance, privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or the deployment of ransomware.

The system is currently not responsive and potentially offline, limiting further analysis at this time. 

File Artefacts:
-----------------
File Path: C:\Users\Pete\Documents\ScreenConnect\Temp\Hide-Mouse-on-blankscreen.exe
SHA256: 3b4ce39ae45d42dc3ed0160db65b90aff4e8d2b4b760ee636315d9814ae056bf

User Accounts:
-----------------
UserName: ATN68139\Pete

Recommendations:
-----------------
- Remove Malicious Client: Investigate and uninstall the malicious ScreenConnect Client (ID: 1132a4b096a44934) and remove the deployed mouse-hiding utility.
- Review All Clients: Review all other ScreenConnect instances (including ID: 24a22b9fc261d141) to confirm they are authorised and legitimate, and ensure all remote access software on the host is secure.
- Enforce an immediate password reset for ATN68139\Pete across all linked systems

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Suspicious RMM: A suspicious remote monitoring and management (RMM) application was detected on this endpoint. These applications may be benign within the environment, or levied by adversaries as a method of persistence. Occasionally software vendors will additionally distribute these RMM's as a portion of their support process. 

Rogue ScreenConnect: Huntress has been tracking a number of malicious threat actors convincing users via email into running malicious ScreenConnect (ConnectWise Control) installers that give the threat actor remote access to the host. 

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete File - path: C:\Users\Pete\Documents\ScreenConnect\Temp\Hide-Mouse-on-blankscreen.exe

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Enroll the user in a security awareness training session to reinforce safe practices in the corporate environment.
- Audit host/environment and ensure this application is allowed and anticipated on this host.

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-08-21 22:32:16 UTC, Huntress observed the following users are compromised:
tabadmin (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1133)
jeanette (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1108)

The compromised user 'jeanette' appears to have logged in from the IP 192.168.10.50 (Host name: DESKTOP-K1PKL6P) to the endpoint ATN79684 at approximately 2025-08-21 19:28:27. 

This user was then observed logging into the endpoint accounting-DAFF0JD at approximately 2025-08-21 22:04:08 and then utilized PSEXEC. Afterwards, this user gained access to the endpoint DEPCO-DC01 and accessed the tabadmin account. Cobalt Strike was utilized during this intrusion, in addition to disabling security features.

It is likely that the initial access occurred through SonicWall. Huntress recommends engaging with incident response as soon as possible in addition to the remediation section. Mass isolation has been deployed to prevent further malicious activity.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Command and Control: Command and Control (C2) consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.

Powershell Scriptblock - Potential Cobalt Strike Loader: A Cobalt Strike Loader is used to stage/inject the beacon for additional post-exploitation/C2 activity.

Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Defense Evasion: Defense Evasion consists of techniques that adversaries use to avoid detection throughout their compromise. Techniques used for defense evasion include uninstalling/disabling security software, clearing logs, or obfuscating/encrypting data and scripts. Adversaries also leverage and abuse trusted processes to hide and masquerade their malware. 

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Reboot the host to terminate malicious processes & sever any potentially outstanding connections. 
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident. 
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- We recommend taking the following steps to respond to this incident:
 
- Disable SSLVPN until this subset of remediations can be accomplished: 
    - Ensure SonicOS is updated to version 7.3 or greater.
    - Rotate all credentials for all users on the SonicWall SSLVPN. 
    - Audit organization LDAP SSLVPN Default User Groups configuration.
    - Disable/reenable TOTP/MFA for relevant accounts. 
    - You can read more about this set of remediations here: https://www.sonicwall.com/support/notices/gen-7-and-newer-sonicwall-firewalls-sslvpn-recent-threat-activity/250804095336430

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105150/infection_reports/1659489#remediations-tab

Investigative Summary
---------------------
[WARNING]
Please review this incident report to understand what was identified before remediating. There may be unknown malicious processes, files, or other changes made to the host that remain undetected. Restoring from a known good backup or clean OS install is the only way to ensure a complete host-level remediation. 

Evidence suggests that on 2025-03-05 at 13:17 UTC, the user 'Kwork' executed a malicious powershell script that reached out to the IP '64[.]94[.]84[.]217' to download additional malicious payloads. 

This led to a malicious install of node.exe in the directory 'C:\Users\KWork\AppData\Roaming\node-v22.11.0-win-x64\', this has been added to assisted remediations for removal. 

Due to the severity of this incident, this host has been isolated to prevent further ingress. 

Huntress detected the following:
- Powershell Downloader : Huntress analysts found a malicious powershell command used to download a payload from a remote server. This is likely being used to deploy malware onto the victim machine. 
- Powershell Payload : This is a Powershell command which extracts a malicious file stored in the registry and executes it. The malicious file is used to spread Ursnif, a banking trojan, which is capable of web browser manipulation, web injections, man-in-the-browser functionality, form grabbing, screen capture, and hidden VNC and SOCKS proxy attacks.

Malicious Downloader / Malicious Download: Malicious activity has resulted in the download and execution of an additional malicious payload(s).

Investigative Summary
---------------------
Huntress has investigated alerts for this host. Due to the nature of these alerts, the host has been isolated.

Evidence suggests the user account "tabadmin" has been compromised and on 2025-08-23  at 11:10:40  the user account "tabadmin" (S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1133) laterally moved to the host from the machine "DEPCO-DC01 ” with Internal IP address "192.168.10.50"

Evidence also suggests the user “tabadmin” attempted to deploy Cobalt Strike malware on the host via PowerShell execution

Evidence suggests that Defender attempted to remove the malicious Cobalt Strike malware, but was unable to block its execution

To prevent further malicious activity, Huntress has deployed mass isolation across all hosts in the org.

Huntress recommends reviewing and actioning the remediations in the previous report as well as those in this report to effectively respond to this incident and permanently evict the adversary.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Command and Control: Command and Control (C2) consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.

The Huntress SOC identified the Malware Family as Cobalt Strike.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Reboot the Host - remediation: A reboot is required to complete the remediation plan

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Reboot the host to terminate malicious processes & sever any potentially outstanding connections. 
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident. 
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105150/infection_reports/1662705#remediations-tab

At 2025-07-30 20:47:28 UTC, Huntress observed user "Administrator"(S-1-5-21-3430465753-3475791500-118270442-500) executing PowerShell.exe via WinrsHost.exe to execute encoded PowerShell. This encoded PowerShell downloads a script for the IP "https://' + '10.4.20.18' + '/resources/scripts/win/cleanup_installation.ps1". In addition, the user vertikal6(S-1-5-21-248130535-2730770219-3953355308-2162) was observed creating the user nutanix and adding it to the local administrators group.

The user 'vertikal6' was also observed disabling the Windows firewall via netsh. Huntress has isolated the host has a precaution.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Malicious Downloader / Malicious Download: Malicious activity has resulted in the download and execution of an additional malicious payload(s). 

Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

New User Creation: Threat actors will often create new users on an endpoint during an attack in an effort to go undetected.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Kill Process - path: C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe + pid: 12236
- Kill Process - path: C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe + pid: 5960
- Reboot the Host - remediation: A reboot is required to complete the remediation plan

Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/103348/infection_reports/1622309#remediations-tab

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------

Backdoor: A backdoor refers to any method in which a threat actor gains unauthorized access to a system by bypassing normal security mechanisms.

Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise.

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-07-23 15:54:13 UTC, Huntress observed user "mdillon" executing a malicious process igfxSDK.exe.

Evidence suggests the user "mdillon" interacted with the malicious file "igfxSDK.exe" which is part of "Lunar" malware, which is known to be malicious. We highly suggest resetting all user credentials and restoring from a known good backup. 

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Backdoor IFEO: A backdoor using IFEO (Image File Execution Options) is a technique that allows threat actors to maintain access to a compromised system. IFEO is a Windows feature that lets developers specify a debugger to use when a program crashes, however, threat actors can misuse this feature to launch their own malicious programs instead of the intended debugger.

The Huntress SOC identified the Malware Families as QakBot and Dridex.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:

Analyze this Huntress EDR incident report and extract every Indicator of Compromise:

Investigative Summary
---------------------
At 2025-08-21 22:32:16 UTC, Huntress observed the following users are compromised:
tabadmin (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1133)
jeanette (SID: S-1-5-21-3426696237-1254496141-3661801895-1108)

The compromised user 'jeanette' appears to have logged in from the IP 192.168.10.50 (Host name: DESKTOP-K1PKL6P) to the endpoint ATN79684 at approximately 2025-08-21 19:28:27.

This user was then observed logging into the endpoint accounting-DAFF0JD at approximately 2025-08-21 22:04:08 and then utilized PSEXEC. Afterwards, this user gained access to the endpoint DEPCO-DC01 and accessed the tabadmin account. Cobalt Strike was utilized during this intrusion, in addition to disabling security features.

It is likely that the initial access occurred through SonicWall. Huntress recommends engaging with incident response as soon as possible in addition to the remediation section. Mass isolation has been deployed to prevent further malicious activity.

Threat Descriptions:
-------------------
Lateral Movement: Lateral movement involves the threat actor pivoting across machines. 

Enumeration (Active directory, Network, Cloud, etc): Enumeration allows a threat actor to identity users, groups, privileges, and assets that can facilitate further ingress and compromise. 

Defense Evasion: Defense Evasion consists of techniques that adversaries use to avoid detection throughout their compromise. Techniques used for defense evasion include uninstalling/disabling security software, clearing logs, or obfuscating/encrypting data and scripts. Adversaries also leverage and abuse trusted processes to hide and masquerade their malware. 

Privilege Escalation: Privilege Escalation consists of techniques that adversaries use to gain higher-level permissions on a system or network. Adversaries can often enter and explore a network with unprivileged access but require elevated permissions to follow through on their objectives. Common approaches are to take advantage of system weaknesses, misconfigurations, and vulnerabilities. Examples of elevated access include:  SYSTEM/root level local administrator user account with admin-like access user accounts with access to specific system or perform specific function These techniques often overlap with Persistence techniques, as OS features that let an adversary persist can execute in an elevated context.

Disabling or Modifying Security Tools on Endpoint: The threat actor has disabled or modified security tooling in an attempt to bypass controls and further compromise the network.

Command and Control: Command and Control (C2) consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.

Remediations:
-------------
Manual Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC are highly recommended remediation actions to be conducted by your team before resolving the incident in the Huntress Platform:
- Consider enacting incident response procedures to assist in fully scoping this security incident.
- Reboot the host to terminate malicious processes & sever any potentially outstanding connections. 
- Audit affected directories/files for additional suspicious files and remove those found. 
- Ensure endpoint anti-virus solutions are deployed and are functioning properly. Audit these services for alerts or warnings on affected hosts.
- Consider re-imaging this host or restoring from a known-good baseline to ensure total remediation of this security incident. 
- Disable and/or rotate credentials for any affected user accounts.
- Audit network perimeter device logs such as firewalls, remote desktop services, etc. for anomalous/unauthorized access.
- Audit local endpoint logging on affected hosts for anomalous access times or unanticipated activity.
- Deploy the Huntress agent to all endpoints in the organization to increase visibility across the network for the Huntress SOC.
- We recommend taking the following steps to respond to this incident: - Disable SSLVPN until this subset of remediations can be accomplished: - Ensure SonicOS is updated to version 7.3 or greater. - Rotate all credentials for all users on the SonicWall SSLVPN. - Audit organization LDAP SSLVPN Default User Groups configuration. - Disable/reenable TOTP/MFA for relevant accounts. - You can read more about this set of remediations here: https://www.sonicwall.com/support/notices/gen-7-and-newer-sonicwall-firewalls-sslvpn-recent-threat-activity/250804095336430

All remediations provided can be found in the Huntress Platform: Incident Report: https://tabinc.huntress.io/org/105150/infection_reports/1659496#remediations-tab

---------------------
Huntress has investigated alerts for this host. Due to the nature of these alerts, the host has been isolated.

Evidence suggests the host has been compromised and on 2025-05-17 at 09:54:23 UTC  a user running with SYSTEM privileges performed credential dumping on both this host "ATN51256" and the host "ATN64140".

Unfortunately both hosts are currently offline, limiting further investigation.

We have deployed mass isolation across the network, to deny the adversary further ingress, and we have the following guidance:

- Consider implementing your Incident Response procedures to fully scope the incident.

- Review internal log sources to identify potential unauthorized access.
- Ensure that the Huntress agent is deployed to all desired hosts within the environment.

Investigative Summary
---------------------
Huntress has investigated alerts for the host "ATN82204"

Evidence suggests that, while browsing the website "hxxps[://]nexusvalve[.]com/contact-us" with Google Chrome, the user "JRowe" (S-1-5-21-1849904498-4039813688-2514114501-5629) clicked on a malicious advert and downloaded a malicious Javascript file "Update 7985.js" and on 2025-02-20 at 13:33:15 UTC they executed the malicious file, infecting the host with the SocGholish malware.

To prevent further malicious activity, Huntress has isolated the host.

Huntress recommends restoring from a known good backup prior to the time of infection .

Huntress also recommends enrolling the user "JRowe" onto security awareness training to prevent similar incidents from occurring in the future. 


[WARNING]
Please review this incident report to understand what was identified before remediating. There may be unknown malicious processes, files, or other changes made to the host that remain undetected. Restoring from a known good backup or clean OS install is the only way to ensure a complete host-level remediation. While the Assisted Remediation service is an option, it will only remove specific items documented in this report.

Huntress detected the following:
- SocGholish / FakeUpdates : SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, is used by threat actors to deliver malicious payloads by tricking users to run malware that is disguised as a legitimate software update.
- Hidden NetSupport Install : This remote desktop application from https://www.netsupportsoftware.com/ is running from a non-standard location. The application is normally located in "c:\program files" and it has been renamed to blend in. Given the odd location of the software and since there are other remote access tools installed on the host this application seems out of place.


Remediation Instructions
------------------------
- restore from a known good backup prior to the time of infection (on 2025-02-20 at 13:33:15 UTC ).
- enroll the user "JRowe" onto security awareness training.

To remediate, perform the actions below:
- Remove the "c:\users\jrowe\downloads\update 7985.js" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Remove the "c:\programdata\bjkaj810\client32.ini" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Remove the "c:\programdata\bjkaj810\client32.exe" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Audit the "c:\programdata\bjkaj810" directory for other suspicious files; remove the directory if not required.

*** NOTE: The user profile for "jrowe" will need to be loaded to view the following registry keys/values (either have the user log in or load the hive manually):
- Remove the "(Default)" value from the "HKU\S-1-5-21-1849904498-4039813688-2514114501-5629\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" registry key.
